chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Convey appoints Jim Hansen as Chief Product Officer and Britta Hormann as Chief of Staff to strengthen executive leadership team - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Convey appoints Jim Hansen as Chief Product Officer and Britta Hormann as Chief of Staff to strengthen executive leadership team  Business Wire

articles.title: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure - SDxCentral
articles.snippet: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure  SDxCentral

articles.title: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching - HousingWire
articles.snippet: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching  HousingWire

post1.text: The biggest leadership mistake scaling companies make is hiring executives to solve today's problems instead of tomorrow's. When you're pushing from $50M toward $500M, the org chart you need in three years looks nothing like the one keeping the lights on right now. Companies like Buffini and Ciena are making bold executive moves precisely because they understand this gap. Here is what that means in practice for growth-stage leaders: First, your leadership team should be slightly ahead of your revenue, not a reflection of it. Hiring to your current size keeps you stuck in it. Second, titles matter less than decision rights. A new Chief of Staff or CPO only adds value if the authority structure around them is clear from day one. Third, culture does not scale by accident. The leaders you bring in carry norms that either reinforce or erode what you have built. What would your leadership team need to look like in three years to actually get you there?

post2.text: A recent piece in HousingWire caught our attention. Buffini and Company just added three senior executives specifically to scale their coaching operations. That move is deliberate, and it tells a story most growth-stage companies miss. When you are scaling a service-driven business, leadership capacity is the ceiling. You cannot outgrow your org chart through willpower alone. At Autobahn Consultants, we see this pattern constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. The ones who stall are not lacking strategy. They are lacking the right people in the right seats with the right authority to execute it. If your growth plan does not include a hard look at your leadership structure, the plan has a gap. Read the full article here and ask yourself whether your executive team is built for where you are going, not just where you are. https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMicEFVX3lxTE80SmJLNXdFS1VDcE9QNXQ2YzNFUDNUSzItcWljNWEyb2NEUVJ0UzdweEIzaVZyclAyWk5fcW54Nlo2U21GSVVoMHFJaVpLSFdaMU1tMXRIWU8wMTJvc3RJNTM5VkhoaVFxZnZFM0VlWVU?oc=5

post3.text: Companies like Ciena and Buffini are making high-profile executive hires to scale. The instinct is right. The execution is where most companies stumble. Chapter 1 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business: Navigating the Road from $50 Million to $500 Million and Beyond, is titled Lead Like Lives Depend On It. Co-author Katherine Slain makes a case that stops most leaders in their tracks: the leadership behaviors that got your company to $50M are often the exact behaviors that will prevent it from reaching $500M. It is not about working harder or hiring more people. It is about fundamentally rethinking what leadership looks like at each stage of growth. There is a specific framework in this chapter that helps leadership teams identify where they are operating below the level the business actually needs. We will be sharing more as the launch approaches. Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M treat executive hiring as a reaction to pain, not a strategy for growth. By the time the gap in leadership capacity becomes obvious, you have already lost months of momentum. The companies that scale fastest build their leadership teams ahead of their revenue targets, not behind them. Here is what that looks like in practice.
posts.email_subject: Your leadership team is a year behind

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: Interview: Harlem Local Darius Ross, From Homelessness To Entrepreneurial Success - Harlem World Magazine
articles.snippet: Interview: Harlem Local Darius Ross, From Homelessness To Entrepreneurial Success  Harlem World Magazine

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: From startup to exit: Deepak Hathiramani’s playbook for GovCon entrepreneurs - The American Bazaar
articles.snippet: From startup to exit: Deepak Hathiramani’s playbook for GovCon entrepreneurs  The American Bazaar

post1.text:
Most business owners think a great exit is about timing the market. It is not. It is about engineering your multiple years before you ever talk to a buyer.

The U.S. Chamber of Commerce recently outlined how exit planning works in practice. What most summaries leave out is the part that actually moves the needle: buyers are not paying for your revenue, they are paying for predictability, transferability, and margin quality.

Here is what leaders at the $50M to $500M level need to understand:

First, customer concentration is a silent multiple-killer. If one client represents more than 20 percent of revenue, expect a discount at the table.

Second, EBITDA is the headline, but the quality of earnings is the story. Buyers will recast your financials. Know what they will find before they do.

Third, management dependency on the founder is priced in. If the business cannot run without you for 90 days, it cannot command a premium multiple.

Your exit is not an event. It is the result of decisions made right now.

What would a sophisticated buyer see as your biggest liability today?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The American Bazaar profiles Deepak Hathiramani's journey from building a government contracting firm to executing a successful exit. His approach was deliberate and methodical, not reactive.

That distinction matters enormously for scaling companies.

At Autobahn Consultants, we work with leaders navigating the $50M to $500M range, and the pattern is consistent: founders who treat their exit as a destination they plan toward outperform those who treat it as an opportunity they stumble into. Hathiramani's playbook reflects exactly that mindset.

If you are in a growth stage and have not mapped your exit variables yet, the cost of waiting is real. Multiples are not static, and the market does not wait for you to get ready.

Read the full piece and start asking the right questions now.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMipwFBVV95cUxQZmViR0NLelR2NGl2Ui1iamZ4TS02MTNWcmpELVkwTDBpankxVW5zQko5dHg0UndfQnFHRXZtZGJhN3FPVkE3Tl8zZWRlRXJBU0cyY2UyakpPd1ZmZzNMU2tFOHZoV1RrNXVOZi1TclBOM0pMVFZSNUozcFZHTWtPdmljdm5NcDZyY0Q2NDc1eV9yU2dnSVN3SFZlckg2RF8ydlg1ZGVQYw

post3.text:
The U.S. Chamber of Commerce recently published a guide on building an exit strategy for your business. It covers the basics well. But Chapter 2 of our upcoming book goes further.

In Rock Your Business: Navigating the Road from $50 Million to $500 Million and Beyond, chapter authors Paul Belair and George Hohman tackle exactly what the general guidance leaves on the table. The chapter is titled Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit, and it addresses something most owners never consider until it is too late: your multiple is not assigned at closing, it is built or destroyed in the years leading up to it.

Belair and Hohman reveal the specific levers that sophisticated acquirers actually pull when they assess a target company. One of them will likely surprise you.

Rock Your Business -- available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most business owners spend years growing revenue and almost no time engineering their exit. That gap is expensive. The difference between a good exit and a great one is rarely timing or luck. It comes down to a handful of structural decisions made 24 to 36 months before any buyer ever sits across the table. Here is what the $50M to $500M leaders who maximize their multiple actually do differently.
posts.email_subject: Your exit multiple is built now, not at close

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Uniserve Advances National Digital Infrastructure Strategy with $1.8 Million Committed Growth Credit Facility - TradingView
articles.snippet: Uniserve Advances National Digital Infrastructure Strategy with $1.8 Million Committed Growth Credit Facility  TradingView

articles.title: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan - CUInsight
articles.snippet: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan  CUInsight

articles.title: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It - Forbes
articles.snippet: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It  Forbes

post1.text:
Most leadership teams don't fail at strategy. They fail at the moment strategy meets reality.

A Forbes analysis on strategy execution breakdown confirms what we see constantly with scaling companies: the plan is rarely the problem. The gap between decision and action is.

Here is what $50M+ leaders need to hear:

1. Ownership without accountability is theater. Someone has to hold the number, the deadline, and the consequence. Not a committee.

2. Quarterly reviews are too slow for fast-moving companies. If you are only checking progress four times a year, you are already behind.

3. Strategy dies in translation. What the executive team agrees to in the boardroom and what the frontline executes six months later are often two different things. Closing that gap is a communication discipline, not a planning one.

Scaling from $50M to $500M is not a strategy problem. It is an execution culture problem.

So here is the question worth sitting with: If your leadership team walked out of your last planning session, could every person describe the top three priorities in exactly the same words?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Forbes breaks down exactly why leadership teams fall apart when it comes to executing strategy, and it is worth a read for any operator trying to scale past $50M.

The core finding is something we see in our work every day at Autobahn Consultants: companies invest enormous energy building strategic plans and almost none building the execution infrastructure to support them.

For scaling companies, this is not a soft problem. It is a revenue problem. Misaligned execution slows growth, burns good people, and creates organizational drift that compounds the bigger you get.

The fix is not a better plan. It is clearer ownership, tighter feedback loops, and leaders who treat execution as the strategy.

If your team is staring at a plan that is not moving, this article is a useful starting point.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxOSXpNczJQdVBKTWlEV0dsby1FaEFGUVg5X1B3Zk9qUGI2bDJMSUxydGhRdURFUUdERXhBRmNzUndTVXhITTREMzMxdk9faE5rUldabk9ld2p2dHdfeXJjVEpMckJ6dFZNckFkTkk5ZmJ5MHRVSm5iSXo2WGlwc0NoeTBvcUYxTF9pbUNmaDB4LUdlNk9pMll0Y01BRTNVYzJObzRrV3pCN19scHYtdThYcHJVUVFYbjFMbkE

post3.text:
Strategy execution failures are not new. But the patterns behind them are more predictable than most leaders want to admit.

In Chapter 3 of our upcoming book Rock Your Business, titled "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," authors Rafa Loyo and Jim Randall draw on hard-won experience across wildly different industries to show why the same execution breakdowns show up whether you are moving consumer goods or enterprise services.

One of the sharpest insights in the chapter: the businesses that scale successfully are not the ones with the most sophisticated plans. They are the ones that built systems to keep the plan alive after the offsite ends.

That distinction sounds simple. The implications for how you structure accountability and communication are anything but.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M don't stall because the strategy was wrong. They stall because no one built the infrastructure to execute it. There is a difference between having a plan and having a system that keeps the plan alive when the pressure is on. That gap is where growth dies, and it is entirely fixable.

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: How to Double B2B Sales Growth - Bain & Company
articles.snippet: How to Double B2B Sales Growth  Bain & Company

post1.text:
Most companies above $50M don't have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There's a difference. Habits are what your team does when no one is watching. Strategy is what your team does when the market shifts and the old playbook stops working.

Bain & Company recently found that doubling B2B sales growth comes down to a small number of deliberate structural choices, not harder work or bigger teams. That finding should stop every growth-stage CEO cold.

Here's what we see consistently with scaling companies:

1. Revenue is growing but no one can explain exactly why. That's luck, not strategy.
2. The sales leader owns the number but not the process. That gap kills repeatability.
3. Personalization is treated as a marketing problem when it's actually a sales architecture problem.

If your company crossed $50M on founder relationships and raw hustle, congratulations. But that same approach will actively prevent you from reaching $200M.

At what point does your leadership team actually audit whether your sales motion is intentional or just inherited?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Bain & Company breaks down what separates B2B companies that double their sales growth from those that stagnate. The findings are not complicated, but they are humbling.

The companies that win are making deliberate choices about where to compete, how to deploy their sales talent, and how to connect their go-to-market motion to actual customer behavior. The ones that struggle are mostly running harder on the same treadmill.

For companies scaling from $50M toward $500M, this is the exact inflection point where instinct stops being enough. The sales strategy that got you here is not the one that gets you there.

If your sales approach has not been formally reviewed in the last 12 months, that is worth a conversation.

Read the full Bain piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMieEFVX3lxTE9KUjR4bFFkR0FPV0NzcF96cEVjVExiZDhMNFVSTHZfYk10UEtuYW8xVmh5blI5a2VSck4ySjhQTTZpQzNBQ3JRWVE3VjhjcEx3SVM2LUpQRmJELUJPS3psdExNdVRKaUpGYVJ6dmM4M3l2blJCSHAybA

post3.text:
There is a chapter in our upcoming book that opens with a line that has stopped more than a few executives mid-sentence.

Chapter 4 of Rock Your Business is called "Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation." It was written by Deb and Brandon, and the title is not a metaphor. It is math.

The research coming out of firms like Bain confirms what Deb and Brandon lay out in detail: growth-stage companies routinely confuse sales activity with sales strategy. The two are not the same thing, and the gap between them has a dollar amount attached to it.

Chapter 4 walks leaders through how to diagnose which one they actually have, and what to do when the answer is uncomfortable.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your company did not reach $50M by accident. But the instincts and relationships that drove that growth are quietly working against you now. Research from Bain confirms what scaling companies keep learning the hard way: there is a meaningful and measurable difference between a sales motion that happened and a sales strategy that was built. Here is what that gap is actually costing you.
posts.email_subject: Your sales strategy might not exist

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture - Fierce Pharma
articles.snippet: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture  Fierce Pharma

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

articles.title: Why Culture Is a Business Investment, Not a Brand Expense - AdvisorHub
articles.snippet: Why Culture Is a Business Investment, Not a Brand Expense  AdvisorHub

post1.text:
Your brand is not your logo. It is not your tagline. It is not the color palette your agency charged you $40,000 to develop.

Your brand is what your company smells like when nobody is performing for the camera.

JBL recently made a decisive shift, moving its marketing investment away from campaigns and toward culture. The result is a brand that customers feel before they can articulate why they trust it. That is not an accident. That is architecture.

For companies scaling past $50 million, three things tend to get exposed fast:

1. Your internal culture and your external marketing are telling different stories. Customers notice the gap before your leadership team does.

2. Campaign thinking is transactional. Culture thinking compounds. One generates a spike. The other builds equity that survives a bad quarter.

3. The brands that scale to $500 million do not spend more on marketing. They spend more on clarity about who they actually are.

So here is the real question for leaders in this growth stage: If your best customers described your company culture to a stranger, would it match what your marketing says about you?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators broke down why JBL is deliberately moving its marketing strategy away from individual campaigns and toward something more durable: culture.

This matters deeply for companies in the $50M to $500M growth range, and here is why.

At that scale, campaign-driven marketing creates a constant pressure to perform without building anything permanent. You win a news cycle and then start over. Culture-driven marketing, by contrast, gives your sales team, your channel partners, and your customers a shared story they can carry without a media budget behind it.

At Autobahn, we see this distinction separate the companies that plateau from the ones that break through.

If you are a growth-stage leader thinking about where your next marketing dollar should go, this article is worth your time.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VEJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw

post3.text:
What does your marketing smell like?

That is not a hypothetical. It is the central question driving Chapter 5 of Rock Your Business, written by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West.

As companies scale from $50 million toward $500 million and beyond, marketing often becomes the first place where internal confusion becomes visible to the outside world. Messaging fragments. Positioning drifts. The brand that worked at $20 million quietly stops working, and leadership is usually the last to know.

Kayli and Yasmin lay out a framework for diagnosing exactly where your brand is breaking down and how to rebuild it around something your whole organization can actually execute, not just your marketing department.

The insight that will stick with you: your brand is less about what you say and far more about what your company consistently does when no one is watching.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling companies have a marketing problem they are misdiagnosing. They assume they need better campaigns, a sharper tagline, or a bigger ad budget. What they actually have is a brand that no longer reflects who they are or where they are going. When your internal culture and your external marketing tell different stories, customers feel the gap long before your leadership team sees it. Here is how to close it.

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: Ashoka Gurujala Joins 66degrees as Director of Talent Acquisition to Lead AI, Cloud & Data Hiring - hrtoday.in
articles.snippet: Ashoka Gurujala Joins 66degrees as Director of Talent Acquisition to Lead AI, Cloud & Data Hiring  hrtoday.in

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

articles.title: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner  Business Wire

post1.text:
Most growing companies treat hiring as a volume problem. It is not. It is a precision problem.

SHRM recently reported that the new standard for hiring in 2026 is precision over scale. Companies that keep chasing headcount without a clear talent architecture are not scaling. They are accumulating cost.

Here is what that means for leaders running $50M to $500M businesses:

1. Your hiring process should be built around the three or four roles that actually drive revenue and margin. Every other position gets staffed around those.

2. Speed to hire is not a virtue when it outpaces your ability to onboard and integrate. A fast bad hire costs you more than a slow good one.

3. Your talent strategy needs to be written down, reviewed quarterly, and owned by someone with real authority. Not HR as an afterthought. HR as infrastructure.

The companies pulling ahead right now are not hiring more. They are hiring smarter, with tighter criteria and longer planning horizons.

What is the one role in your organization where a wrong hire would set you back 12 months?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM lays out a clear argument: the era of scaling headcount to solve business problems is over. In 2026, precision in hiring is the competitive edge.

This matters enormously for companies in the $50M to $500M growth corridor. At that stage, a misaligned senior hire does not just cost salary. It costs momentum, team trust, and sometimes an entire strategic initiative.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders who have learned this the hard way. The ones who scale well are not the fastest hirers. They are the most intentional ones. They define the role before they post it, align it to a specific business outcome, and hold the bar even when the timeline is uncomfortable.

If your hiring process still starts with a job description instead of a business problem, it is time to rethink the engine.

Read the full SHRM article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aU0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
The conversation around precision hiring is not new to us. It is exactly what Chapter 6 of our upcoming book addresses head-on.

In Rock Your Business: Navigating the Road from $50 Million to $500 Million and Beyond, Chapter 6 is called The Talent Engine. Written by Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West, this chapter tackles what actually breaks down inside a company's people strategy as it scales.

One of the ideas they surface: most mid-market companies do not have a talent problem. They have a talent system problem. The hiring, development, and retention practices that worked at $20M are quietly working against you at $100M.

The framework they lay out gives leaders a way to diagnose exactly where the engine is misfiring before it costs them a quarter or a key person.

Rock Your Business is available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: The companies stalling between $50M and $500M are rarely failing because of strategy. They are failing because the people infrastructure never kept pace with the ambition. Precision hiring, intentional talent architecture, and a system built for scale are not HR concerns. They are the engine. Here is what leaders at this stage need to rethink first.

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026 - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

post1.text:
Most companies don't suffer from too few metrics. They suffer from too many.

There's a quiet epidemic in mid-market businesses: leadership teams drowning in dashboards while the real performance signals get buried. Oracle NetSuite recently catalogued 30 financial metrics worth tracking. Thirty. For most $50M to $500M companies, that list isn't a roadmap. It's a trap.

Here's what high-growth operators actually need to know:

1. More KPIs don't create more clarity. They create more meetings about the metrics instead of action on the business.

2. The right number of KPIs for a leadership team is almost always fewer than they think. Ruthless prioritization is a competitive advantage.

3. Lagging indicators tell you what happened. If your dashboard is full of them, you're steering by looking in the rearview mirror.

The companies that scale well pick the metrics that predict outcomes, not just report them. They hold those numbers accountable and ignore the rest.

How many KPIs does your leadership team actually review every week, and how many of those drive a real decision?

post2.text:
A recent piece in CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen) outlines the KPIs manufacturers should track to improve company performance. It is a solid breakdown of the numbers that matter on the plant floor and in the financials.

But here is what it does not cover: what happens when a manufacturer scaling past $50M tries to run every department off the same KPI playbook.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. The metrics that got a company to $50M are rarely the ones that get it to $200M. New complexity demands new signals. And leaders who do not rethink their measurement systems mid-scale end up optimizing for the wrong outcomes at exactly the wrong time.

If you lead a manufacturing or industrial business in a growth phase, this article is worth a read as a starting point. Then ask yourself which metrics your team has outgrown.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxNc3hGVVVxVnMwVEpLcmxaNWJzaURJbERjczZaSDhlcnhvOFlHX1VZU0tETkctQjFqRVhZVkNtcFNoUWtfSmRhSmU5N3pYc25IVzlfTEs5TDFZWk1RTmhZd2JzSkpPSXVmQndRRHRBM29CaDBoWnVWRXcxMnBhWkcwT3dmWTAwSDFSeS1hYzFtRUNtd05nWE9PX25nbzRfc2ZzSjVFVURMY0hMRkdVMmtOdjVOYUluUXJIcEE

post3.text:
There is a reason Chapter 7 of our upcoming book is called "Death by KPIs."

Authored by Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, this chapter tackles one of the most common and most costly mistakes companies make as they scale through the $50M to $500M range: building a measurement culture that measures everything and improves nothing.

The article landscape right now is full of lists. 14 tools. 30 metrics. More dashboards. More data. But as the chapter makes clear, the problem for most scaling companies is not access to numbers. It is knowing which numbers actually deserve leadership attention, and building the discipline to act on only those.

There is a framework inside this chapter that will change how your leadership team runs its weekly reviews. We will leave it at that for now.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your company is not underperforming because you lack data. It is underperforming because the wrong data has everyone's attention. Mid-market companies scaling past $50M almost universally make the same mistake: they add KPIs instead of sharpening them. The result is a leadership team that spends more time reviewing metrics than running the business. That stops here.
posts.email_subject: Are your KPIs killing your growth?

articles.title: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era - Harvard Business Review
articles.snippet: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era  Harvard Business Review

articles.title: Rochester executives share playbook for AI implementation - Rochester Business Journal
articles.snippet: Rochester executives share playbook for AI implementation  Rochester Business Journal

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

post1.text: Most executives are measuring AI the wrong way. They are tracking cost savings and task completion rates while the real competitive advantage is quietly building elsewhere. Harvard Business Review recently made the case that traditional performance metrics are simply not built for what AI actually changes inside an organization. Here is what that means for leaders running $50M+ companies. First, the executives who win with AI are not the ones who automate the most tasks. They are the ones who redeploy the human attention that gets freed up. Second, AI does not improve a broken process. It accelerates it. If your workflows are messy, AI will surface that faster than any consultant will. Third, your managers need new coaching skills now, not after your next AI rollout. Managing human-plus-AI output is a fundamentally different job than managing humans alone. The companies scaling toward $500M are treating AI as an organizational design challenge, not a software procurement decision. What metric is your team actually using to measure whether AI is making your people more effective?

post2.text: A recent piece in the New York Times covered a growing trend among busy executives who are deploying AI versions of themselves to handle meetings, answer questions, and preserve their thinking for others to access on demand. It is a fascinating development, and for scaling companies it raises a more urgent question than most leaders are asking. At Autobahn, we work with businesses moving from $50M toward $500M and beyond. At that stage, the bottleneck is almost always leadership bandwidth. The CEO becomes the constraint. AI twins and digital proxies are not novelty tools. For the right operator, they are infrastructure. The leaders who figure out how to scale their judgment, not just their headcount, are the ones who reach the next level without burning out. Read the full piece here and think about where your own bandwidth is the ceiling: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ

post3.text: Rochester executives recently shared their AI implementation playbook in the Rochester Business Journal, and one theme stood out: the companies seeing real results are not just buying tools. They are changing how their leadership teams see and process information. That idea sits at the core of Chapter 8 in our upcoming book, Rock Your Business. The chapter is called The Third Monitor Theory, written by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain. The premise is simple but the implications run deep. Most leaders are already overwhelmed by what is on their first two screens. The third monitor represents something different entirely. It is about how AI can expand what a leader is capable of seeing in their business, not just what they can automate. There is a specific framework inside this chapter that changes how growth-stage operators think about their own attention and decision-making. Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: AI is not a productivity tool. For companies scaling past $50M, it is a leadership stress test. The executives getting real results are not automating tasks. They are rethinking how they deploy human judgment, redesign workflows, and scale their own decision-making capacity. Here is what that actually looks like inside a growth-stage business.
posts.email_subject: AI is exposing your real bottleneck

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top - Forbes Australia
articles.snippet: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top  Forbes Australia

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

post1.text:
The leaders most likely to derail your company's growth are not the incompetent ones. They are the ones who are quietly convinced they are.

Imposter syndrome does not disappear when you hit $50M in revenue. Forbes Australia recently explored how this phenomenon actually intensifies at the top, following high achievers precisely because they have the most to lose and the fewest peers to normalize the feeling with them.

Here is what that means for leaders scaling toward $500M:

First, self-doubt at scale becomes an organizational problem. When a CEO hesitates to make bold moves because they feel unqualified, the entire company slows down waiting for a decision that never comes.

Second, the leaders who perform best are not the ones without doubt. They are the ones who have built systems and teams that function regardless of their internal state on any given Tuesday.

Third, naming this dynamic inside your leadership team removes its power faster than any mindset coaching will.

So here is the real question: Is your company's next growth ceiling a strategy problem, or is it sitting inside someone's head at the executive table?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Seattle University's publication outlines six practical ways leaders can work through imposter syndrome when stepping into new roles.

Good advice. But here is what it does not address: at the $50M to $500M growth stage, this is not a new-leader problem. It is a scaling-leader problem.

When your title stays the same but the complexity of your job doubles every 18 months, the psychological gap between who you were and who the role now demands widens fast. That gap is where imposter syndrome does its worst work.

Autobahn works with leaders navigating exactly this. The companies that scale well invest in leadership psychology as deliberately as they invest in operational infrastructure.

Read the full article here and ask yourself where this might be showing up in your own leadership team: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMivwFBVV95cUxPTHlqVjJWbmVSRkRPWnNTLU05TjNYZmVlelFGYUNIYmg1TXlHRmpjUmMtYWx6OEtzSjJIdVNmbGtidllGQzZXZU1kZS1ZTmxONm1JWko3MVdTVjlvczl0MTN4eF91aURLN05SUU5UMUNrbXlBU09kSTczZGItUzNfczZKMUlOZnJqX3hFNXZ6LWV3YVB5SzF3eUZZY1VVajBJWG5tRFZiSDhhT3NaVXppVkYtWDlRZUJOTVNLbVk1WQ

post3.text:
Fast Company recently argued that we should stop using the term imposter syndrome altogether, suggesting the label itself may do more harm than good.

Chapter 9 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, takes this conversation somewhere most leadership books never go.

Titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?" and written by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, this chapter reframes the entire conversation for leaders operating at serious scale.

One thing it makes clear: the goal is not to eliminate self-doubt. The goal is to stop letting it make decisions for you.

What that looks like inside a company pushing toward $500M is more nuanced, and more actionable, than any checklist will tell you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leader scaling past $50M has a version of the same quiet fear: that someone is about to figure out they do not actually belong at this level. What most growth frameworks miss is that imposter syndrome does not shrink as your company grows. It gets more sophisticated, and more expensive, when left unaddressed at the executive level.
posts.email_subject: The self-doubt that's stalling your growth

articles.title: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs - Fortune
articles.snippet: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs  Fortune

articles.title: HPU Student Entrepreneurs Earn $30,000 in Business Plan Competition - High Point University
articles.snippet: HPU Student Entrepreneurs Earn $30,000 in Business Plan Competition  High Point University

articles.title: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy. - Shopify
articles.snippet: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy.  Shopify

post1.text:
The most dangerous moment in a founder's career is not when the company is struggling. It is when everything is working.

Morgan Stanley recently examined what happens to entrepreneurs after a major liquidity event. The pattern is striking: many high-performers who built companies worth hundreds of millions report feeling lost, purposeless, and unprepared for what comes next. Not because they lacked money. Because they had never designed a life outside the business.

For leaders scaling past $50M, three things tend to get deferred indefinitely: a clear personal vision beyond revenue targets, intentional boundaries that protect physical and cognitive capacity, and relationships that exist outside of professional utility.

These are not soft concerns. They are operational risks. A leader running on empty makes worse decisions. A leader with no identity outside the company cannot delegate effectively because letting go feels like self-erasure.

The business plan gets updated every quarter. When did you last update your life plan?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune, drawing on Morgan Stanley research, maps out what entrepreneurs actually face after selling their businesses. The financial preparation is usually solid. The personal preparation almost never is.

For companies scaling from $50M toward $500M, this is not just a post-exit problem. Leaders who have not thought carefully about what they are building toward, not just what they are building, tend to make growth decisions that serve the company in the short term and hollow out their own leadership capacity over time.

At Autobahn, we work with executives who are operationally excellent and personally adrift. The two are connected more than most want to admit.

If you are in a high-growth phase right now, this article is worth your time: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
Morgan Stanley's research on entrepreneurs after a liquidity event points to something we have seen repeatedly with scaling companies: the leader's personal life plan is always the last thing to get built and the first thing to collapse under pressure.

Chapter 10 of Rock Your Business, titled 'Coming Up for Air,' addresses this directly. Authored by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain, the chapter makes a case that most leaders scaling past $50M have never formally designed the life they are working toward, only the business.

One insight worth sitting with: life planning is not a reward you earn after hitting a revenue milestone. It is a discipline that makes hitting the milestone sustainable and worth something when you get there.

Rock Your Business — available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most executives scaling past $50M have a five-year business plan and no life plan. That imbalance does not stay contained to personal time. It shows up in decision-making, delegation, and the kind of leadership that either builds culture or quietly corrodes it. Here is what high-growth leaders need to understand about designing a life before the business designs one for them.
posts.email_subject: Your business plan is solid. Your life plan?
